# Transcrição: 05- 5.0 - Omission

- **Fonte:** 05- 5.0 - Omission
- **Duração:** 09:03
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:04]

All right, the next step is omission, and it might be the hardest thing for you to do. Essentially, we have to remove things from your story that don't belong, but you might think belong. Those are those items that are near and dear to your heart. The fact that your mother had the bluest eyes there ever was. The fact that your home was like this beautiful red house with a yellow door, all of these details that mean something to you, but mean absolutely nothing to the story. We have to get rid of them. The phrase that's often used is kill your darlings, often attributed to William Faulkner, but it was actually first said by Sir William Quiller Couch, which is a great name. Anytime the word couch is in your name, you should deserve credit for the things that you say. Kill your darlings, and we call them darlings because they often mean something to us. But the recognition and awareness that we must have is that these details that are personally relevant to us are often irrelevant to the audience. We tell stories first to ourselves, and when we tell the story to ourselves, we can include all of the details we want. We can include every little tiny bit that means something to us. But as soon as we're taking that story and offering it to an audience, we have to think about that thesis statement. What serves the thesis statement? What belongs in the story that helps us get to the end in the clearest way possible and what needs to go? Here's three things in the story that I'm working on that need to go from my story, even though all three of them mean a great deal to me. The first one is the McDonald's Play Place where this situation is taking place. I used to manage this restaurant for six years of my life. I worked in this restaurant. I power washed this play place that my children are going to be trapped in multiple times. I hosted birthday parties in this play place. The manager who served us the food is a Chinese immigrant who spoke no English when I met her, and I helped her become the manager of the restaurant on this day. I feel intimately attached to this McDonald's play place. And yet that fact does not belong in the story. It has nothing to do with my children and the way they're growing up and the way they're separating from me, my belief that they're going to be okay in life. It's an important fact to me, but it clouds the story completely. So I don't talk about it at all. Even though in my head, it's so important to me. It's one of my darlings. The second fact that's going to go is the fact that on this day, it's actually Father's Day, which you would think sort of heightens the story to a certain degree. And yet still I think it kind of clouds the story. But even more than that, it's Father's Day. And just before Charlie gets stuck in the tube, I get a phone call. One of my former students, Father, died on this day.

## [03:04 - 06:07]

I get a phone call that tells me that a man that I knew for two years, because I've taught two of his children suddenly died of a heart attack. And on Father's Day, two kids wake up without a father and a woman wakes up without a husband. You can understand how this moment that takes place on the phone sort of heightens all of my emotions when I go into that play place and suddenly find Charlie stuck in the tube. But can you also see how this moment, this realization that a man I once knew has died on this day? Can you see how that would cloud the story? Can you see how the story would become more about a man and his death than a moment in a tube? For me, it is a critical moment on that day. The most important thing that happens that day is I discover that man died, yet it doesn't fit in the story. Now it might be another story that I tell someday. I could tell the story of the day on Father's Day when I discover that a father has died and I am still alive and my children are lucky and two children in the world are not. I could tell that story, but that's a separate story. The fact that it's Father's Day and that a man I know has died is important to me. It is the biggest darling of the day. And yet it must go away because it's going to cloud the story and make the story about something that I don't need it to be about. I don't want the story to be about the death of a man. I want the story to be about a father recognizing that his kids are going to be okay. And so that has to fall away too. The third detail that I leave out of the story is the fact that my daughter has autism. Now on this particular day, I don't know she has autism. In fact, I don't even suspect it. It won't be another four years before she actually does testing and we discover this to be true. In many storytellers minds, this would be a critical detail to include. They would say a ridiculous statement like, little did I know that four years later, never say that. Never say little did I know that four years later because we tell stories about moments. We don't tell stories about moments where we have vision to the future. I had no vision to the future. I was existing in that day at that time with a certain mental framework. And that's the only thing that belongs in my story. The fact that eventually I'll discover that some of the problems that Clara has with the play place and the fact she's only eating pancakes and four other foods, all of those details will eventually be explained partially by autism. But on that day, I have no explanation. I just think my daughter is a little bit of a pain in the butt. And that's what I need to bring to the story. I need to bring the reality of that day and not any future awareness to the story. So when we know something about the future that is relevant to the moment we leave the future out, that's a darling we kill because nobody wants to hear a story about, here's a moment in my life and all the stuff I found out after, they just want to hear, here's

## [06:07 - 09:03]

a moment in my life and what it meant to me. So those are three enormous things. And I'm leaving all of them out of my story. It kills me to leave them out of the story. It is killing my darlings. It is the hardest thing for storytellers to do because so much of our stories are important to us. But in the end, we start with a thesis statement because the thesis statement directs us. It tells us what must be included in the story and what must be discarded. And we have to honor that thesis statement because we're not reporting on our lives. If I was reporting on my life, I would tell you, I used to work in this McDonald's. I know it well. It's Father's Day. A man died on this day. My daughter has autism. That's reporting on my life. It's boring. It's not crafty. It doesn't convey a message. It makes the story less memorable. It's a giant pile of facts instead of a carefully curated story chosen from the facts that are most relevant to the story. So you save those stories. You save those darlings for the moments when you're having dinner with a friend and you want to share everything. You save the darlings for the moment when you want to tell yourself your story while you're walking your dog. You save those moments for when you're sitting your kids down and saying, listen, I want you to know everything about my life. Throw it all in. But when you're standing in front of an audience, when you're speaking to a group of people in a conference room, when you're trying to land a point and a message and meaning and connection and all the things we do with stories, we have to kill our darlings. So you have to do that now. You have to look at your story and ask yourself, what details am I including that I'm only including because I like them, because I think they're cool, because they mean something to me, because I really want you to know they have to go away. You have to ask yourself, do they fit the thesis statement of my story? Do they help me get from the beginning to the end with clarity? Or am I just putting them in there because I like them? If you're putting them in there because you like them, they have to go. It's going to hurt. It's challenging. It's painful. It doesn't mean they're gone forever. You're not excising them from your life. You're simply excising them from this version of the story, the story that you're going to prepare to tell other people to land message and meaning and connection and heart and all of the things we're trying to do. So go to your story now. Think about each one of the scenes and ask yourself, what belongs in the scene and what do I have to kill? It won't be easy, but it's probably the most important thing you can do when it comes to bringing great clarity and meaning to your story. Go ahead and do that now. Good luck.
